content:
# Final Review Proposal
**Producer:** agent:claude/claude-opus-4-7-max

## Summary

Reviewed the IterativeMinimal flow end to end: plan+implement loop, stable-diff exit, resume recovery via `resume_terminal_iterative_stage_result`, sidecar parsed-payload persistence with retry + in-memory fallback, git and non-git fingerprinting, and the cross-scope workspace/project validation. Regression coverage for the pre-existing Minimal flow (`minimal_flow_remains_single_pass_without_iteration_events`) and for the other flows looks sound, and the additive dispatch gate keeps Standard/QuickDev/DocsChange/CiImprovement/Minimal paths untouched. No critical correctness issues found. Flagged two medium-severity hardening concerns: (1) workspace-scope config writes now depend on every project's config being parseable, so one corrupt project config blocks unrelated workspace config changes; (2) the filesystem fingerprint fallback reads entire files into memory with only top-level exclusions, which can OOM on monorepos or large data files when git is unavailable.

## Amendments

1. Workspace-level `EffectiveConfig::set` now unconditionally invokes `validate_workspace_iterative_minimal_settings`, which calls `candidate_project_ids_for_iterative_validation` to walk both `audit/projects` and `live/projects` and then calls `FileSystem::read_project_config` on every project discovered. `read_project_config` returns `AppError::CorruptRecord` on malformed TOML (and bubbles up other I/O errors), so a single corrupt or schema-invalid project `config.toml` will fail the validator and block ALL workspace-level config writes — even writes to unrelated keys such as `workflow.max_cycles`. Pre-change, workspace config writes and project config reads were independent. This also adds O(projects) directory-read + TOML-parse I/O to every workspace config write. Recommend either (a) scoping the walk/validation to runs where iterative_minimal keys actually change, or (b) treating a per-project read failure as a skip-with-warning rather than a hard error so a broken project config can't wedge the workspace.
   Rationale: Pre-change, an operator with one broken project could still repair workspace config. Post-change, they must first fix the broken project — a real regression in recoverability for a shared-workspace tool.
2. `hash_workspace_entries` (the filesystem fingerprint fallback used when git is unavailable) reads each discovered file in full via `std::fs::read(&path)?` before hashing. The exclusion list only filters top-level `target`, `target-final`, and `result*`; nested monorepo build directories (e.g. `packages/foo/target/`, `crates/bar/target/`), large tracked data files (fixtures, media), or lockfiles buried anywhere below the root will be read wholesale into memory. On a resource-constrained CI container without git, this can drive the process OOM or cause pathological runtime on each iteration's fingerprint. The git path is unaffected because `git diff --binary` streams through git. Recommend either (a) streaming/hashing files in chunks rather than `fs::read`, (b) enforcing a per-file size cap (hash the first N bytes + file size), and/or (c) expanding the exclusion list to match `.gitignore`-style nested patterns so nested `target/` directories are skipped.
   Rationale: IterativeMinimal fingerprints the workspace every iteration. A repo with large nested artifacts under a non-git environment would silently OOM or stall CI, and the failure mode is hard to diagnose because it only triggers when git is missing.
